authoritative_sources.title: Rules on mortgage servicing
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CFPB is the primary U.S. consumer-financial regulator for mortgage servicing. This official rules hub points to the governing Regulation X and Regulation Z servicing, payment-processing, error-resolution, and periodic-statement requirements that constrain servicing operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Servicing intake, disclosures, and exception handling

authoritative_sources.title: Electronic Fund Transfers FAQs
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CFPB is authoritative for Regulation E implementation. Its official FAQs are directly relevant to disputed transfers, investigations, provisional credit treatment, and consumer-facing timing duties in payment operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Charge, dispute, and payment exception intake
used_for: required evidence before correction or release

authoritative_sources.title: Assessing Compliance with BSA Regulatory Requirements - Suspicious Activity Reporting
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The FFIEC BSA/AML manual is an official interagency examination reference used by U.S. banking regulators. It is authoritative for escalation boundaries, SAR confidentiality, and when operations work must stop and move to AML or fraud ownership.
used_for: refusal of unsupported innocence or fraud conclusions

authoritative_sources.title: FFIEC BSA/AML Appendices - Appendix P – BSA Record Retention Requirements
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official FFIEC appendix defines record-retention expectations for BSA/AML documentation, including SAR-supporting documentation, and therefore constrains evidence handling and case retention behavior.

authoritative_sources.title: Risk Management Manual of Examination Policies - Section 2.1 Operations - Internal Routine and Controls
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FDIC examination policy is authoritative for operational controls in insured depository institutions, including proofing, reconciliations, exception processing, segregation, and documentation expectations used in account and payment operations.

authoritative_sources.title: ACH Quality and Risk Management Topics
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public servicing, payments, or policy workflow rules
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Nacha governs the ACH Network rules used across U.S. payment operations. This public rules material is directly relevant to unauthorized returns, return-code distinctions, and operational exception terminology for payment posting and reconciliation.

authoritative_sources.title: Unfair Claims Settlement Practices Act
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public servicing, payments, or policy workflow rules
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NAIC model-law content is a recognized public insurance-operating framework used by state insurance regulators. It is directly relevant to prompt investigation, fair settlement, and communication standards in claims and policy servicing workflows.

authoritative_sources.title: Uniform Individual Accident and Sickness Policy Provisions Law
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public servicing, payments, or policy workflow rules
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This NAIC model law is a recognized public baseline for insurance premium grace periods, reinstatement, and notice-sensitive billing behavior. It is directly relevant to premium billing, lapse-risk, and consumer-impact handling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Premium billing, notices, and lapse-sensitive servicing

workflow_stages.name: Product and servicing-path classification
workflow_stages.description: Identify whether the case is deposit servicing, lending or mortgage servicing, payment operations, policy servicing, or claims-adjacent operations and confirm which public rules and tenant records govern the request.
source_classes: Public servicing, payments, or policy workflow rules

workflow_stages.name: Charge, posting, premium, and dispute support validation
workflow_stages.description: Validate that account, policy, transaction, payment, notice, and approval records support a financial action or require refusal due to missing prerequisites.

workflow_stages.name: Reconciliation, denial, and return review
workflow_stages.description: Compare system-of-record truth, settlement or remittance records, and downstream ledger outcomes to identify matched items, denials, returns, suspense items, and unresolved variances.
source_classes: Public servicing, payments, or policy workflow rules

workflow_stages.name: Fraud, AML, and consumer-impact exception routing
workflow_stages.description: Stop in-lane handling and escalate when suspicious activity, SAR-sensitive evidence, customer-harm risk, or disclosure-sensitive obligations materially change the outcome.

workflow_stages.name: Boundary and authority escalation
workflow_stages.description: Escalate when legal, licensed, underwriting, claims-coverage, write-off approval, accounting-signoff, or jurisdiction-specific policy interpretation is required.
source_classes: Public servicing, payments, or policy workflow rules

artifacts: Account, policy, loan-servicing, application, claim, and transaction records
Payment-hub, settlement, remittance, ACH return, and suspense-account records
KYC, AML, fraud, dispute, and suspicious-activity case artifacts
Servicing notes, approval histories, disclosure notices, periodic statements, and customer communication logs
General-ledger extracts, reconciliation workpapers, exception queues, and variance logs
Tenant policy extracts, product terms, approval matrices, and jurisdiction-specific procedures
systems_of_record: Payment hub, ACH processor, or settlement platform
Case management, CRM, and document management systems
Fraud monitoring and AML case systems
General ledger, reconciliation tooling, and regulatory reporting systems

allowed: Validate whether retrieved records support a charge, posting, payment application, billing, premium, reconciliation, or escalation outcome.
Prepare traceable summaries, reconciliation notes, and exception packets with explicit uncertainty and owner routing.
Separate product-line workflows when public rules or tenant controls prohibit blended financial treatment.
escalate: Product-specific disclosure, grace-period, or servicing rules are missing and would change the result
Account, payment, policy, or ledger records disagree
Fraud, AML, suspicious activity, or customer-harm signals are present
Denial, dispute, write-off, or adjustment requires accounting, compliance, underwriting, or legal ownership
refuse: Requests to create or alter account, policy, payment, or claim facts without source evidence
Requests for regulated advice, claims coverage decisions, underwriting outcome, write-off approval, accounting treatment, or legal interpretation
Requests to treat the financial-services industry label as cosmetic and skip product, jurisdiction, or source-of-truth context

domain_failure_modes: Posting or adjusting a financial outcome that is not supported by authoritative account, policy, payment, or approval records
Treating banking, lending, payments, and insurance servicing rules as interchangeable and generating unsupported financial treatment
Silently correcting denials, returned items, or reconciliation mismatches without traceable evidence and owner approval
Proceeding when fraud, AML, or consumer-impact signals require specialized review
Using generic finance terminology that hides product-servicing artifacts such as periodic statements, grace-period notices, suspicious-activity cases, ACH returns, or policy administration records

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Disclosure or reconciliation conflict that requires escalation

unresolved_ambiguities.detail: Exact error-resolution timelines, grace periods, cancellation notices, and statement requirements vary by product line and jurisdiction. The pack treats these as tenant-required retrieval facts and refuses confident synthesis when they are missing.

NAIC model laws are a public operating baseline, but state adoption differs. The pack uses NAIC for terminology and control framing while requiring tenant jurisdiction and current state rules before final premium or claims-servicing handling.

notes: The source mix covers workflow, evidence, terminology, and failure handling rather than only one dimension.
No blocking ambiguity remains for validated packaging because tenant-specific product and jurisdiction facts are explicit prerequisites, not hidden assumptions.
